perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall notify Disclosing Party immediately if Receiving Party has reason to believe that any person who has had access to the Proprietary Information (including Receiving Party or any of its Representatives) has violated or intends to violate the terms of this Agreement or otherwise disclose any Proprietary Information in violation of the terms hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall notify Disclosing Party within 30 days if Receiving Party has reason to believe that any person who has had access to the Proprietary Information (including Receiving Party or any of its Representatives) has violated or intends to violate the terms of this Agreement or otherwise disclose any Proprietary Information in violation of the terms hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification timeline from "immediately" to "within 30 days" could impede the Disclosing Party's ability to take swift legal action to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to protect trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA allows for ex parte seizure orders in cases of trade secret misappropriation. Delaying notification to the Disclosing Party by 30 days may prevent them from acting quickly enough to obtain such an order, thus hindering their ability to effectively protect their trade secrets and potentially causing irreparable harm. Immediate notification is essential for timely legal action.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The change from "immediately" to "within 30 days" directly contradicts the intent of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) as it impedes the Disclosing Party's ability to take swift action to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that the DTSA allows for ex parte seizure orders, and delaying notification hinders the ability to obtain such an order in a timely manner. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 18 USC 1836, outlines civil proceedings available to an owner of a trade secret when misappropriation occurs. The original text ensured immediate notification, allowing the Disclosing Party to act quickly, whereas the modified text introduces a significant delay, weakening their ability to effectively use the legal options available under 18 USC 1836.

perturbation.original_text: Interested Party agrees that neither it nor its affiliates will at any time until the one year anniversary of the Effective Date, directly or indirectly, employ or solicit for employment (i) any key technical or management personnel of the Company that has first been introduced by the Company to the Interested Party in connection with the Proposed Transaction or who was otherwise substantively involved in the discussions of the Proposed Transaction or (ii) any other person who is now employed as an officer of the other party or any of its affiliates; provided, that the foregoing restrictions hall not be deemed to prohibit Interested Party or its Representatives from making general public solicitations for employment for any position or from employing any employee of the Company who either responds to such a general solicitation for employment or otherwise contacts Interested Party on his or her own initiative and without solicitation by Interested Party in contravention of the above restriction.
perturbation.changed_text: Interested Party agrees that neither it nor its affiliates will at any time until the 30-day anniversary of the Effective Date, directly or indirectly, employ or solicit for employment (i) any key technical or management personnel of the Company that has first been introduced by the Company to the Interested Party in connection with the Proposed Transaction or who was otherwise substantively involved in the discussions of the Proposed Transaction or (ii) any other person who is now employed as an officer of the other party or any of its affiliates; provided, that the foregoing restrictions hall not be deemed to prohibit Interested Party or its Representatives from making general public solicitations for employment for any position or from employing any employee of the Company who either responds to such a general solicitation for employment or otherwise contacts Interested Party on his or her own initiative and without solicitation by Interested Party in contravention of the above restriction.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to 30 days may violate California law, which disallows non-solicitation agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business unless it falls under specific exceptions, which typically require longer periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrowly tailored non-solicitation clauses related to the sale of a business may be enforceable, a short 30-day restriction on soliciting employees is unlikely to be considered a reasonable restraint necessary to protect trade secrets or the goodwill of a business, particularly given California's strong policy against restraints of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-solicitation period from one year to 30 days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight that California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrow exceptions exist, a 30-day restriction is unlikely to be considered reasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The reduction to 30 days creates an inconsistency because it likely violates California's prohibition on restraints of trade, thus the contractual term is unenforceable under state law.

perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise provided herein, the obligations of this Agreement, including the restrictions on disclosure and use, shall terminate on the second anniversary of the Effective Date; provided that Sections 2.5, 2.6, and 2.7 and Articles 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 and 9 shall survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as otherwise provided herein, the obligations of this Agreement, including the restrictions on disclosure and use, shall terminate 90 days after the Effective Date; provided that Sections 2.5, 2.6, and 2.7 and Articles 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 and 9 shall survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 90 days creates a high risk of trade secret misappropriation. This violates the Defend Trade Secrets Act which protects trade secrets for as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to protect trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value. Setting a confidentiality period of only 90 days provides an insufficient time frame for the trade secret to be protected, potentially enabling easy misappropriation and thus contradicting the intent and protections offered by the DTSA. Trade secrets often maintain their value for significantly longer periods, rendering a 90-day protection inadequate.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period to 90 days, which contradicts the intent of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as explained in both the Perturbation and Law Explanations. The DTSA aims to protect trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value. A 90-day period is insufficient to ensure this protection, potentially enabling misappropriation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 USC 1836, outlines civil proceedings for trade secret misappropriation, indicating the legal framework for protecting trade secrets. The modification weakens the protection of trade secrets, making the contract non-compliant with the DTSA's purpose and creating a high risk of trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or other communication required or permitted to be delivered under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed effectively given: (i) upon personal delivery to the party to be notified; (ii) when sent by confirmed telex or Facsimile if sent during normal business hours of the recipient, if not, then on the next business day; (iii) three (3) days after having been sent by registered or certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid; or (iv) one(1) day after deposit with a nationally recognized overnight courier, specifying next day delivery, with written verification of receipt, to the address or facsimile number set forth beneath the name of each party below (or to such other addressor facsimile number as such party may designate by five (5) days advance written notice to the other party hereto).
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or other communication required or permitted to be delivered under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed effectively given: (i) upon personal delivery to the party to be notified; (ii) when sent by smoke signal; (iii) ten (10) days after having been sent by registered or certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid; or (iv) five (5) days after deposit with a nationally recognized overnight courier, specifying next day delivery, with written verification of receipt, to the address or facsimile number set forth beneath the name of each party below (or to such other addressor facsimile number as such party may designate by five (5) days advance written notice to the other party hereto).
perturbation.explanation: Replacing confirmed telex or facsimile with "smoke signal" is unreasonable and undermines the enforceability of the notice provision. The use of certified mail also is extended to ten days after mailing, and five days after deposit.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Reasonableness and Good Faith
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, requiring parties to act honestly and reasonably. Replacing a standard method of communication (facsimile/telex) with an absurd one (smoke signal) and extending the mail delivery time to ten and five days violates this covenant. It demonstrates a lack of intention to provide effective and timely notice, undermining the fairness and enforceability of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 'smoke signal' as a method of communication, replacing 'confirmed telex or Facsimile,' which is a standard and reliable method. The Law Explanation states this undermines the covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards.' Using smoke signals and significantly extending mail delivery times (ten and five days), instead of the original three and one, demonstrates a lack of intention to provide effective and timely notice, contrasting with the required good faith.
